paper.title: The photospheres of the hottest fastest stars in the Galaxy

extraction.summary: This paper performs NLTE atmospheric model analysis of the three hottest hypervelocity stars. These three stars (J0546+0836, J0927-6335, J1332-3541) were discovered by El-Badry et al. (2023, Open Journal of Astrophysics, 6, 28) as D^6 (Type Ia supernova survivor) candidates, with space velocities ranging 1500-2800 km/s, far exceeding the Milky Way escape velocity. The paper confirms J0546+0836 as a [WC]-PG1159 transition type (oxygen-rich atmosphere, Teff=95000K), J0927-6335 as PG1159 type (carbon-oxygen atmosphere, Teff=60000K), and J1332-3541 as a DAO-type white dwarf (hydrogen-rich atmosphere, Teff=65657K). The paper confirms the D^6 scenario is a plausible origin hypothesis for J0546+0836 and J0927-6335, but the hydrogen-rich atmosphere of J1332-3541 is difficult to explain.

method_chain.summary: NLTE model atmosphere analysis (TMAW/TMAP code packages) determining atmospheric parameters (Teff, log g, chemical composition), stellar parameters (mass, radius, luminosity), and spectral classification of the three hypervelocity stars. Spectral data from Keck-I LRIS (R~1500).

source_refs.context: Abstract: NLTE model atmosphere analyses of three hottest hypervelocity stars using TMAW/TMAP code packages
Sect.2: model atmospheres and analysis strategy using TMAW/TMAP

method_chain.summary: Spectroscopic distances for the three stars determined via SED fitting (chisq method) combining Gaia DR2/EDR3, Pan-STARRS/SkyMapper/SDSS photometric data.

source_refs.context: Sect.2: SED fitting with chisq method for spectroscopic distances

method_chain.summary: Radial velocities adopted from El-Badry et al. (2023, Open Journal of Astrophysics, 6, 28). For example, the radial velocity of J0546+0836 is approximately 1350 km/s.
inputs: El-Badry et al. (2023) radial velocity measurements

source_refs.context: spectrum shifted to rest wavelength assuming radial velocity of 1350 km/s

method_chain.summary: Hypervelocity star classification and D^6 (Type Ia supernova survivor) origin scenario adopted from El-Badry et al. (2023). This paper evaluates the classification and confirms the D^6 scenario is plausible for J0546+0836 and J0927-6335.
inputs: El-Badry et al. (2023) classification and D^6 scenario

source_refs.context: Abstract: identified as runaways from Type Ia supernovae

method_chain.summary: Total space velocity range (1500-2800 km/s) adopted from El-Badry et al. (2023). These velocities far exceed the Milky Way escape velocity (~500-600 km/s), indicating these stars are in a gravitationally unbound state with respect to the Galaxy.

source_refs.context: space velocities between ~1500-2800 km/s
Section 3 (J0546+0836): dedicated section for this star, LRIS spectrum analysis

inclusion_assessment.summary: J0546+0836 is one of the three hypervelocity stars studied in this paper, with space velocities in the range 1500-2800 km/s (from El-Badry et al. 2023). The paper refers to it as a hypervelocity star and a Type Ia supernova runaway. Radial velocity approximately 1350 km/s. The paper discusses it within the D^6 scenario and confirms that this scenario is plausible for it.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: The paper explicitly labels it as a hypervelocity star, with space velocities of 1500-2800 km/s far exceeding the Milky Way escape velocity (~500-600 km/s). Individual velocity and Galactic-unbound status come from El-Badry et al. (2023); this paper builds on that by providing atmospheric parameter analysis and confirming the plausibility of the D^6 origin scenario.

distance.description: Spectroscopic distance from SED fitting with E(B-V)=0.29 mag. Note: Gaia distance is 4.0^+2.3_-1.7 kpc, but paper notes their SED distance is ~4x larger, which would imply tangential velocity of ~5400 km/s that is difficult to explain.

source_refs.context: J0546+0836: SED reproduced with E(B-V)=0.29 mag, spectroscopic distance 15^+4_-8 kpc

radial_velocity.description: Radial velocity used to shift observed spectrum to rest wavelength. Value from El-Badry et al. (2023).

source_refs.context: LRIS spectrum shifted to rest wavelength assuming a radial velocity of 1350 km/s

spectroscopy.description: [WC]-PG1159 transition-type pre-white dwarf with mixed absorption+emission line spectrum. Wind emission lines from O III/O IV, photospheric absorption from O V/O VI.

source_refs.context: Spectroscopically, J0546+0836 is a [WC]-PG1159 transition-type pre-white dwarf

teff.description: Effective temperature from NLTE model atmosphere analysis

log_g.description: Surface gravity (log g) from NLTE analysis

mass.description: Stellar mass from comparison with post-AGB evolutionary tracks. Paper notes log g may be underestimated for this object.

radius.description: Stellar radius derived from log g and mass

abundances.description: Helium abundance upper limit (mass fraction). Star is devoid of helium.

origin_classification.description: Dynamically driven double-degenerate double-detonation (D^6) scenario: Type Ia supernova survivor. Paper confirms this scenario is plausible for J0546+0836.

source_refs.context: Abstract: discuss results in context of D^6 scenario proposed for the origin of these stars
Summary: confirms D^6 scenario is plausible hypothesis for J0546+0836 and J0927-6335

inclusion_assessment.summary: J0927-6335 is the second hypervelocity star studied in this paper, with space velocities of 1500-2800 km/s (El-Badry et al. 2023). PG1159-type white dwarf with carbon-oxygen atmosphere. The paper confirms the D^6 scenario is plausible for it. Velocity far exceeds the Milky Way escape velocity.

source_refs.context: Abstract: space velocities between ~1500-2800 km/s
Abstract: third object (J1332-3541) to be a relatively massive (M=0.89 Msol) hydrogen-rich (DAO) white dwarf

inclusion_assessment.summary: J1332-3541 is the third hypervelocity star studied in this paper, with space velocities of 1500-2800 km/s (El-Badry et al. 2023). DAO-type hydrogen-rich white dwarf with higher mass (0.89 Msol). The paper notes that its hydrogen-rich atmosphere creates tension with the D^6 scenario, and the hydrogen may have been acquired through accretion from the interstellar medium. Velocity far exceeds the Milky Way escape velocity.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Belongs to the same D^6 hypervelocity star sample discovered by El-Badry et al. (2023); the paper explicitly labels it as a hypervelocity star. Space velocities of 1500-2800 km/s far exceed the Milky Way escape velocity. Note, however, that the paper points out tension between its atmospheric composition (hydrogen-rich) and the D^6 scenario.

source_refs.context: Abstract: three hottest hypervelocity stars (space velocities ~1500-2800 km/s)
J1332-3541: hydrogen-dominated DAO WD, D^6 scenario has difficulty explaining atmospheric composition

spectroscopy.description: DAO white dwarf: hydrogen-rich with roughly solar helium abundance, chemically homogeneous atmosphere. Not a DO-to-DA transition object.

source_refs.context: J1332-3541: not a hot helium-rich DO WD as originally proposed, but hydrogen-dominated DAO WD

mass.description: Stellar mass, relatively massive for a WD

spectral_type.description: Spectral type: DAO (hydrogen-rich white dwarf with helium)

abundances.description: Hydrogen abundance (mass fraction). Presence of hydrogen is puzzling for D^6 scenario.

origin_classification.description: D^6 scenario, but hydrogen-rich atmosphere difficult to reconcile. Paper suggests possible H accretion from ISM during fast cruise through the Galaxy.

source_refs.context: J1332-3541: presence of hydrogen is puzzling and difficult to reconcile with D^6 scenario. May have collected H during fast cruise through ISM.

candidate_groups_considered.description: The three hottest hypervelocity stars (J0546+0836, J0927-6335, J1332-3541). Discovered by El-Badry et al. (2023, Open Journal of Astrophysics, 6, 28) as D^6 hypervelocity white dwarf candidates, with space velocities ranging 1500-2800 km/s (far exceeding the Milky Way escape velocity ~500-600 km/s). This paper performs NLTE atmospheric model analysis, determines atmospheric parameters and chemical compositions, and confirms the D^6 scenario is plausible for J0546+0836 and J0927-6335, but notes tension with the hydrogen-rich atmosphere of J1332-3541.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: All three stars are hypervelocity stars discovered by El-Badry et al. (2023), with space velocities of 1500-2800 km/s far exceeding the Milky Way escape velocity ~500-600 km/s, making them clear candidates for Galactic gravitational unbinding. This paper validates these candidates at the atmospheric parameter analysis level and confirms the plausibility of the D^6 origin scenario. All three stars should be included in the HVS candidate catalog.

source_refs.context: Abstract: three hottest hypervelocity stars (space velocities ~1500-2800 km/s), identified as runaways from Type Ia supernovae
Introduction: D^6 model - surviving WD escapes at orbital velocity 1000-2500 km/s
Summary and Discussion: confirms D^6 scenario plausible for J0546+0836 and J0927-6335; J1332-3541 has tension due to H-rich atmosphere
